examples.title: First Fit Memory Allocation
examples.description: Algorithm to allocate memory using First Fit strategy
examples.code:
// First Fit Allocation
// Input: Array of holes (size), Process size request
// Output: Allocation index or -1 if not possible

FUNCTION FirstFit(holes[], processSize)
    n = length(holes)
    
    FOR i = 0 TO n-1 DO
        IF holes[i] >= processSize THEN
            // Allocate here
            allocatedHole = holes[i]
            remaining = allocatedHole - processSize
            
            // Replace current hole with remaining
            holes[i] = remaining
            
            RETURN i  // Return index of allocation
        END IF
    END FOR
    
    RETURN -1  // No suitable hole found
END FUNCTION

// Example usage:
holes = [300, 200, 500, 150, 400]
processes = [210, 180, 320, 100]

FOR EACH p IN processes DO
    index = FirstFit(holes, p)
    IF index != -1 THEN
        PRINT "Process " + p + "KB allocated at hole " + index
    ELSE
        PRINT "Process " + p + "KB cannot be allocated"
    END IF
END FOR
examples.explanation: First Fit scans memory from the beginning and allocates the first hole large enough. It is fast as it doesn't search for the best fit.

examples.title: Best Fit Allocation in C
examples.description: C implementation of Best Fit memory allocation strategy
examples.explanation: Best Fit searches all holes to find the smallest hole that can accommodate the process, minimizing leftover fragment.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n²) if done repeatedly for multiple processes

examples.title: Memory Compaction Simulation
examples.description: Python program demonstrating memory compaction to remove external fragmentation
examples.code:
class MemoryBlock:
    def __init__(self, pid, size, is_free=True):
        self.pid = pid  # None if free
        self.size = size
        self.is_free = is_free
    
    def __repr__(self):
        if self.is_free:
            return f"[HOLE: {self.size}KB]"
        return f"[P{self.pid}: {self.size}KB]"

def print_memory(blocks, label="Memory"):
    print(f"\n{label}:")
    for block in blocks:
        print(block, end=" ")
    print()

def compact_memory(blocks):
    """Perform memory compaction - move all free blocks together"""
    # Separate allocated and free blocks
    allocated = [b for b in blocks if not b.is_free]
    free_blocks = [b for b in blocks if b.is_free]
    
    # Calculate total free memory
    total_free = sum(b.size for b in free_blocks)
    
    # Create compacted memory: all processes first, then one big hole
    compacted = allocated.copy()
    if total_free > 0:
        compacted.append(MemoryBlock(None, total_free, True))
    
    return compacted

# Initial memory state with external fragmentation
memory = [
    MemoryBlock(1, 200),   # Process 1
    MemoryBlock(None, 50), # Hole 1
    MemoryBlock(2, 150),   # Process 2
    MemoryBlock(None, 30), # Hole 2
    MemoryBlock(3, 100),   # Process 3
    MemoryBlock(None, 20), # Hole 3
    MemoryBlock(4, 80),    # Process 4
]

print("=== MEMORY COMPACTION DEMONSTRATION ===")
print_memory(memory, "Before Compaction")

# Try to allocate a 150KB process - will fail due to fragmentation
print("\nAttempting to allocate 150KB process...")
can_allocate = any(b.is_free and b.size >= 150 for b in memory)
print(f"Can allocate: {can_allocate} (External fragmentation!)")

# Perform compaction
compacted = compact_memory(memory)
print_memory(compacted, "After Compaction")

# Now allocation is possible
print("\nAttempting to allocate 150KB process again...")
can_allocate = any(b.is_free and b.size >= 150 for b in compacted)
print(f"Can allocate: {can_allocate}")
examples.explanation: Demonstrates memory compaction: scattered free holes are consolidated into one large block, allowing larger processes to be loaded.

examples.title: Base-Limit Register Address Translation
examples.description: MMU simulation showing address translation and bounds checking
examples.code:
// Memory Management Unit (MMU) Simulation
// Using Base and Limit Registers

CONSTANTS
    MAX_MEMORY = 1024  // 1MB physical memory
    BASE_REG = 0      // Base register value
    LIMIT_REG = 0     // Limit register value

RECORD ProcessControlBlock
    pid
    baseRegister
    limitRegister
    state
END RECORD

VAR memory[0 TO MAX_MEMORY-1]  // Physical memory array
VAR currentProcess  // Currently running process

FUNCTION translateAddress(logicalAddress)
    // Get current process's registers
    base = currentProcess.baseRegister
    limit = currentProcess.limitRegister
    
    // Step 1: Bounds check - is address within limit?
    IF logicalAddress >= limit THEN
        // Address out of bounds - generate trap
        TRAP("SEGMENTATION FAULT: Address exceeds limit")
        RETURN -1
    END IF
    
    // Step 2: Calculate physical address
    physicalAddress = base + logicalAddress
    
    // Step 3: Ensure physical address is valid
    IF physicalAddress >= MAX_MEMORY THEN
        TRAP("MEMORY FAULT: Physical address out of range")
        RETURN -1
    END IF
    
    RETURN physicalAddress
END FUNCTION

// Example: Process P1 loaded at base=100, limit=500
FUNCTION loadProcess(pid, size, code[])
    base = findContiguousSpace(size)  // Find free space
    IF base == -1 THEN
        RETURN FALSE  // No space available
    END IF
    
    // Create PCB
    pcb = createPCB(pid, base, size)
    
    // Load code into memory
    FOR i = 0 TO size-1 DO
        memory[base + i] = code[i]
    END FOR
    
    RETURN TRUE
END FUNCTION

// Example usage:
// loadProcess(P1, 500KB, programCode)
// currentProcess = P1
// logicalAddress = 250 → physicalAddress = 100 + 250 = 350
// logicalAddress = 600 → TRAP (exceeds limit of 500)
examples.explanation: Simulates MMU operation: translates logical addresses to physical using base register, checks bounds using limit register for protection.
